name: Amoxicillin
warn: ⚠ VERIFIED vs Carpenter 6e. Carpenter Rabbit chapter explicitly states: "Do not give any form of penicillin orally to rabbits" — this includes amoxicillin. FATAL via PO in hindgut fermenters & most small rodents — clostridial enterotoxemia. Choose enrofloxacin, TMS, or chloramphenicol instead. ⚠ Ferret: combined with metronidazole + bismuth subsalicylate for Helicobacter (triple therapy).

name: Amoxicillin-Clavulanate
warn: ⚠⚠ Carpenter 6e p.644 RABBIT TOXIC LIST: Amoxicillin-Clavulanate causes "enteritis; enterotoxemia". FATAL enterotoxemia risk in all herbivorous rodents/lagomorphs — NEVER use PO or systemic in rabbit / GP / chinchilla / hamster / gerbil.

name: Ampicillin
warn: ⚠⚠ Carpenter 6e p.644 RABBIT TOXIC LIST: Ampicillin causes "enteritis; enterotoxemia, HIGH RISK especially if given orally". Carpenter Rodent p.563: "Do not use orally in hamsters, guinea pigs, chinchillas — may cause enterocolitis". ⚠ Carpenter Hedgehog: NOT recommended as antimicrobial resistance common. Bird PO absorption poor — IM preferred (Carpenter p.257).

name: Azithromycin
warn: ⚠ Macrolides typically cause enterocolitis in lagomorphs/rodents — but azithromycin is the EXCEPTION: Carpenter 6e p.607 lists explicit rabbit doses for syphilis and pulmonary infections. Use with caution; monitor for diarrhea. ⚠ NON-REGENERATIVE ANEMIA reported in Ball Pythons — monitor PCV on long-term use. ⚠ USE CAUTION IN RENAL DISEASE (Lecture Seminar 53, Vet Council 2026) — azithromycin is renally excreted; reduce dose / extend interval if azotemia present and recheck creatinine within 5–7 days of starting.

name: Cefovecin
use: Long-acting single-injection — but exotic half-lives MUCH shorter than dog/cat
warn: ⚠ Carpenter 6e p.258 (bird) and p.529 (sugar glider): NOT recommended due to short half-life / high interspecies PD variability — do NOT extrapolate dog/cat q14d dosing! ⚠ Ferret PK: q2–3 days actual dosing interval (Carpenter p.659). Hedgehog PK: q5–6 days (p.544). ⚠ Cephalosporins generally cause GI dysbiosis in rabbits & chinchillas.

name: Cephalexin
warn: ⚠⚠ Carpenter 6e p.644 RABBIT TOXIC LIST: Cephalosporins cause "enteritis; enterotoxemia if given orally". p.607: "Oral cephalosporins are not recommended" in rabbits. PARENTERAL (SC 15 mg/kg q12h) is acceptable. ⚠ Hedgehog: may loosen stools (Carpenter p.544).

name: Ceftazidime
use: Pseudomonas, Gram− in reptiles (workhorse), bird respiratory
warn: ⚠ VERIFIED vs Carpenter 6e. ⚠ RENAL PORTAL SYSTEM: ALWAYS inject CRANIAL half (forelimb / pectoral) in birds & reptiles to avoid renal first-pass. Reptile: q48–72h dosing per Carpenter (long half-life). ⚠ Carpenter Reptile note: 90% strength maintained for 120h refrigerated (4°C), 25+ days frozen (−18°C) in 0.2 mL plastic tuberculin syringes. ⚠ In KUVTH retrospective study, all 11 Gram-positive isolates were resistant — Gram-neg drug only.

name: Ceftiofur
warn: Carpenter Bird p.258: NOT recommended in birds with short half-life — many species need q4–8h dosing of regular Naxcel. ⚠ EXCEDE (ceftiofur crystalline free acid) provides much longer half-life — preferred long-acting form. Generally safer in rabbits than oral cephalosporins; monitor stool quality.

name: Chloramphenicol
warn: ⚠ VERIFIED vs Carpenter 6e. ⚠ HUMAN aplastic anemia risk (idiosyncratic, dose-independent) — wear gloves, avoid skin contact. Carpenter Hedgehog: potentially toxic to humans — avoid human contact with medication. ⚠ AVOID in food-producing animals — prohibited by US FDA. ⚠ Bird: not commercially available in US (can be compounded); large PK differences between species, do not extrapolate. ⚠ Reptile: reserve for meningitis or encephalitis caused by susceptible organisms (Carpenter).

name: Doxycycline
use: Mycoplasma, Chlamydia, Bartonella, tick-borne, Psittacosis 45-day protocol
warn: ⚠ VERIFIED vs Carpenter 6e. Tetracyclines (incl. oxytetracycline) toxic in guinea pigs — avoid. ⚠ Carpenter Bird note: 12.5-25 mg/kg PO q12-24h in lorikeets caused ↑ AST, bile acids, hepatocellular damage. ⚠ Cats: always chase with water/food — esophageal stricture risk. ⚠ Avoid in young / pregnant animals (tooth + bone discoloration). ⚠ **COMPOUNDING (Lecture Seminar 53, Vet Council 2026)**: doxycycline syrup is LIGHT-SENSITIVE — store in opaque container, refrigerate, stability ≤ 7 days. Splitting "fish-bone" tablets gives UNEVEN dose per piece — use compounded suspension for accurate small-patient dosing. Al/Ca/Mg/Fe in food/products reduce absorption.

name: Enrofloxacin
warn: ⚠ VERIFIED vs Carpenter 6e (exotic species). ⚠ Cats: > 5 mg/kg → blindness (retinal toxicity, dog/cat reference). ⚠ Carpenter explicitly: SC and IM injections may cause MUSCLE NECROSIS or STERILE ABSCESSES — DILUTE before parenteral use (NaCl or LRS). ⚠ Hedgehog: avoid > 1 dose IM. May cause arthropathies in young animals (dog data); not reported in rabbits at standard doses per Carpenter.

name: Marbofloxacin
warn: ⚠ VERIFIED vs Carpenter 6e. ⚠ Do NOT give during lactation/pregnancy or to growing animals (Carpenter rodent note). Injectable form can be given orally per Carpenter. Lowest MIC of 9 antibiotics tested for rabbit upper respiratory infections (Carpenter).

name: Metronidazole
use: Anaerobes, Giardia, IBD, hepatic encephalopathy, clostridial enterotoxemia
warn: ⚠ VERIFIED vs Carpenter 6e + Lecture Seminar 53 (Vet Council 2026). ⚠ PROLONGED USE → neurotoxicity (ataxia, head tilt, seizure) — esp. cats, ferrets, chinchillas. ⚠⚠ CHINCHILLA: explicitly causes ANOREXIA (Seminar 53) — choose alternative when possible; if mandatory, monitor food intake daily + force-feed if needed. Bitter taste also reduces voluntary intake. ⚠ Sugar glider: CNS toxicity possible at high doses or with hepatic disorder — Carpenter recommends compound at 5 mg/mL tutti-frutti flavor + use benzoate form. Benzoate salt has 1.6× less active drug than HCl — adjust dose. Reptile: limit to 7–10 d. Carpenter Reptile p.145: death in indigo and mountain king snakes at 100 mg/kg.

name: Trimethoprim-Sulfa (TMS)

doses.d: 30 mg/kg PO × 2 wk OR 75 mg/kg PO q5d × 5 wk (with spiramycin/metro for crypto)

warn: ⚠ Carpenter 6e p.644 RABBIT TOXIC LIST footnote: "antibiotic-related colitis in rabbits given... trimethoprim/sulfamethoxazole". Risk of dysbiosis — use with caution in rabbits, monitor stool. ⚠ Avoid in DEHYDRATION or renal dysfunction (Carpenter Reptile p.149). KCS / sicca syndrome in dogs on long-term use. Hepatotoxicity. Maintain hydration. Sulfa hypersensitivity in some Dobermans.

name: Tylosin
warn: ⚠ Carpenter Hedgehog p.545: "do not administer IM — causes muscle necrosis". ⚠ Macrolide dysbiosis risk in small herbivores — CONTRAINDICATED in guinea pig / chinchilla / hamster. RABBIT: BSAVA Rabbit Medicine lists tylosin (with azithromycin) as the SAFER macrolides for rabbit URT (10 mg/kg q12h) — use only if indicated + monitor stool. Erythromycin / clindamycin / lincomycin remain contraindicated in rabbits.

name: Gentamicin
use: Severe Gram− sepsis (last-line). Topical for ophth / abscess.

doses.f: q12–24h (dilute IV w/ saline, give over 20 min)

warn: ⚠ VERIFIED vs Carpenter 6e. NEPHROTOXIC + ototoxic — use only when culture demands. ⚠ Carpenter Rabbit chapter: "Seldom indicated; use with caution." ⚠ Maintain hydration; baseline + recheck SDMA/creatinine. ⚠ RENAL PORTAL SYSTEM: in birds & reptiles ALWAYS inject CRANIAL half (pectoral / forelimb) — caudal injection of nephrotoxic aminoglycoside → direct renal exposure. ⚠ Ferret PO route: use parenteral product orally only for proliferative colitis (does not absorb systemically). ⚠ Sugar Glider: Carpenter does NOT recommend gentamicin — amikacin preferred.

name: Amikacin
use: Resistant Gram−, Pseudomonas, MRSA. Topical wound/abscess.

doses.f: q12h (high peak — once daily equivalent PK)

warn: ⚠ VERIFIED vs Carpenter 6e. Aminoglycoside — nephrotoxic + ototoxic. Best when culture confirms (Pseudomonas 98% S in KUVTH data). ⚠ Carpenter Rabbit: dilute in 4 mL/kg saline for IV, give over 20 min; once-daily dosing increases efficacy AND decreases toxicity. ⚠ RENAL PORTAL: inject CRANIAL half in birds/reptiles. ⚠ PMMA bead (1.25 g/20 g methylmethacrylate) for jaw abscess (rabbit) per Carpenter.

name: Clindamycin
warn: ⚠⚠ ORAL lincosamide = FATAL clostridial enterotoxemia in rabbit/guinea pig/chinchilla/hamster/gerbil — NEVER give PO to hindgut fermenters (Carpenter rabbit toxic table p.644). Rodent route is SC only. Exotic: Ferret p.659, Hedgehog p.544, Sugar Glider p.529.

name: Lincomycin
warn: ⚠⚠ ORAL lincosamide = FATAL clostridial enterotoxemia in hindgut fermenters — never use in rabbit/GP/chinchilla/hamster/gerbil (Carpenter p.644). Exotic: Ferret p.660, Sugar Glider p.529.

name: Cefazolin
warn: ⚠ Parenteral only. ORAL cephalosporins = FATAL enterotoxemia in rabbit/GP/chinchilla/hamster/gerbil (Carpenter p.644). Reptile/chelonian dose p.134.

name: Penicillin G (procaine / benzathine)
warn: ⚠⚠ NEVER give ANY penicillin ORALLY to rabbit/GP/chinchilla/hamster/gerbil — FATAL enterotoxemia (Carpenter p.644: "Do not give any form of penicillin orally to rabbits"). Parenteral pen G used in rabbit/ferret/hedgehog only. Procaine itself may be fatal in rabbits at high dose. Exotic: Rabbit p.609, Ferret p.660, Hedgehog p.545.

name: Oxytetracycline
warn: ⚠ May cause injection-site inflammation (reptile, Carpenter p.139). Avoid in pregnant/young (teeth/bone). Exotic: Ferret p.660, Hedgehog p.545, Reptile p.139.

name: Neomycin
warn: ⚠ ORAL/gut use ONLY — not absorbed, so NOT effective for systemic infection; EXTREMELY nephrotoxic if given parenterally. Possible nephrotoxicity/neuromuscular block even PO if mucosa damaged. Exotic: Ferret p.660.

name: Nitrofurantoin
use: Lower urinary tract infection (concentrates in urine)
warn: ⚠ No exotic-species dose in Carpenter — exotic species omitted (do not extrapolate). For lower-UTI only, not systemic/renal infection. Avoid in renal impairment.

name: Florfenicol
warn: ⚠ No companion-mammal label dose — those rows omitted to avoid fabrication. Fish dose (representative across species) Carpenter p.58.

name: Erythromycin
warn: ⚠⚠ ORAL macrolide = FATAL enterotoxemia in hindgut fermenters (Carpenter p.644). Ferret use = Campylobacter diarrhea. Exotic: Ferret p.659, Bird p.267.

name: Itraconazole
warn: African Grey parrots: anorexia, depression, toxicity at higher doses (Carpenter 6e p.280) — many clinicians halve to ~2.5 mg/kg, this lower dose NOT in Carpenter, off-label. Hepatotoxic — monitor LFTs. Bearded dragons may become anorectic without hepatotoxicity. Compounded formulations may not be bioavailable — use commercial suspension first choice (Carpenter p.279). ⚠⚠ ITRACONAZOLE CAPSULE = COATED PELLETS — do NOT crush, break, or dissolve to make oral suspension (Lecture Seminar 53, Vet Council 2026). The enteric-coated pellets lose bioavailability if crushed. Either swallow capsule whole or use commercial liquid suspension (Sporanox Oral Solution).

name: Fluconazole
use: CNS / urinary mycoses, candidiasis (good CSF penetration)
warn: ⚠ FATAL in budgerigars at 10 mg/kg PO q12h (Carpenter 6e p.278) — also ineffective vs avian gastric yeast at that dose. Use 2–5 mg/kg q24h for most species. Hepatotoxic at high doses.

name: Terbinafine
warn: Carpenter 6e (p.611) states terbinafine has "little activity when used as a single agent" in rabbits — prefer combination therapy; BSAVA Rabbit Medicine gives 100 mg/kg q12h for refractory dermatophytosis. Questionable efficacy for avian aspergillosis as monotherapy.

name: Voriconazole
use: Severe / resistant aspergillosis (better tissue penetration than itraconazole)
warn: ⚠ FATAL in cottonmouth snakes at 5 mg/kg SC — high mortality within 1–2 hr (Carpenter 6e p.142). Voriconazole toxicity reported in multiple penguin species (Carpenter p.282). Hepatotoxic — monitor LFTs. Compounded suspensions stable up to 30 days at room temp.

name: Ketoconazole
warn: ⚠ Hepatotoxic; largely supplanted by itraconazole. Fungistatic — variable yeast/fungus sensitivity. Carpenter p.280 (bird), p.142 (reptile), p.661 (ferret).

name: Nystatin
warn: ⚠ NOT absorbed from the GI tract — for GI/crop candidiasis only, NEVER for systemic mycoses. Carpenter p.281 (bird), p.565 (rodent).

name: Miconazole
warn: ⚠ Topical for local dermatophytosis; in birds often combined with oral itraconazole. Carpenter p.281 (bird), p.142 (reptile).

name: Griseofulvin
warn: ⚠⚠ TERATOGEN — do NOT use in pregnant animals (Carpenter p.546). High-dose bone-marrow suppression (rabbit p.611). ⚠ Caution in cats; CONTRAINDICATED in FIV+ cats (severe neutropenia/pancytopenia). Rarely used now (safer azoles available).

name: Ivermectin
warn: ⚠⚠ LETHAL in chelonians (tortoises, turtles): flaccid paresis + death at 0.025–0.3 mg/kg — Carpenter 6e p.144. AVOID in crocodilians, indigo snakes, skinks. Snakes eating ivermectin-treated rodents may also become toxic. ⚠ Parenteral ivermectin TOXIC in finches and budgerigars (Carpenter p.291). Avoid in MDR1-mutation dogs (Collies, Aussies) — use selamectin. ⚠ Drug interaction with diazepam/tiletamine in reptiles within 10 days.

name: Selamectin
warn: Carpenter rabbit PK data limited at repeat-application 20 mg/kg dose — long-term safety not fully assessed (Carpenter p.614). Safer alternative to fipronil for rabbits.

name: Fenbendazole
warn: ⚠ Bone marrow suppression reported in rabbits and chelonians at high dose (Carpenter rabbit p.613, also benzimidazole class warning p.644). Recommend intratreatment CBC for prolonged use. Anemia/arteritis rarely reported in rabbits. ⚠ Use caution in birds — benzimidazole toxicity (bone-marrow suppression, enteritis) reported in several avian species.

name: Praziquantel
warn: ⚠ Carpenter (bird p.297): Injectable form toxic in finches — depression, death reported. Use PO in finches.

name: Pyrantel Pamoate
warn: Generally well tolerated. Use as part of empirical anthelmintic combo.

name: Ponazuril
warn: ⚠ Carpenter p.147: Ponazuril at 20 mg/kg PO in red-footed tortoises did NOT achieve effective anticoccidial concentrations. ⚠ Rabbit dose for E. cuniculi NOT in Carpenter 6e rabbit chapter — off-label, anecdotal use; fenbendazole 20 mg/kg × 28d is the Carpenter-cited treatment for rabbit E. cuniculi.

name: Toltrazuril
warn: ⚠ Carpenter (bird p.304): 2.5% solution is very alkaline — do NOT gavage directly into crop. Bitter taste — mix with cola/soft drink to improve palatability. Drug detected in eggs after multiple dosing in birds. Rabbit coccidiosis dose from BSAVA Rabbit Medicine: 2.5–5 mg/kg PO q24h × 2d, repeat in 14d.

name: Fipronil
warn: ⚠⚠ Carpenter 6e p.613 (and rabbit toxic-drug list p.644) state Fipronil is CONTRAINDICATED in rabbits — "neurologic disease and death; blocks GABA receptors in CNS by preventing chloride ion uptake." ⚠ Reptile toxicity reported (Carpenter p.144) — beware alcohol carrier. Spray ferret/rodent application only; AVOID rabbits and untested reptile species.

name: Permethrin

doses.sp: Reptile — Lizard/Snake (environmental treatment ONLY, NOT on animal)
doses.d: 1 sec spray/ft²; dry before returning animal

warn: ⚠⚠ ABSOLUTELY LETHAL in cats — status epilepticus, hyperthermia, death within hours. Cats CANNOT glucuronidate pyrethroids. NEVER use any permethrin in any house where cats live. ⚠ Carpenter 6e p.146: DEATH reported in snakes that had permethrin applied directly on body. Use ONLY for environmental treatment in reptile husbandry (wash immediately if accidentally on skin). Antidote: bathe in dish soap, methocarbamol IV/PO, lipid emulsion 20%, supportive care.

name: Imidacloprid + Moxidectin
warn: Carpenter p.144: For reptiles, start at low dose to avoid side effects (1 Asian grass lizard out of 4 died after treatment). Safer alternative to fipronil for rabbits and exotic mammals. Avoid in collies / MDR1-mutation dogs.

name: Fluralaner
warn: Long duration of action — single dose lasts ~12 weeks. Rare neurological signs (ataxia, seizure) reported in dogs/cats — use caution with seizure history.

name: Albendazole
warn: ⚠ Carpenter 6e p.612 (rabbit) "use cautiously, deaths have been reported". TERATOGENIC — never in pregnancy/first trimester. Bone marrow suppression possible (esp. cats, pigeons, rabbits, chelonians) — CBC before prolonged use. ⚠ Carpenter (bird) — may be toxic in keas, some Columbiformes at 50–100 mg/kg.

name: Nitazoxanide
warn: GI upset common. Yellow urine/sclera (benign metabolite color). ⚠ Rabbit/rodent doses not from Carpenter 6e — off-label.

name: Sulfadimethoxine
warn: Maintain hydration to prevent crystalluria. Sulfa hypersensitivity in some dog breeds (Dobermans). Bone marrow suppression with prolonged use.

name: Emodepside + Praziquantel
warn: ⚠ Avoid in pregnancy (emodepside may affect fetal development — Carpenter p.613). Aquatic turtles kept dry 48h after application (p.143).

name: Diclazuril
warn: ⚠ Some Eimeria resistance in poultry — rotate for long-term prevention. Rabbit alt: 1 ppm in feed. Carpenter p.288 (bird), p.612 (rabbit).

name: Moxidectin
warn: ⚠ Ferret dose = heartworm adulticide (Carpenter p.663). Rabbit 0.2 mg/kg PO repeat ×10d for psoroptic mange (p.614). Caution at high doses in MDR1 breeds.

name: Lufenuron
warn: ⚠ Larvicide only — does NOT kill adult fleas; combine with adulticide + environmental control. Carpenter p.662 (ferret), p.614 (rabbit).

name: Amitraz
warn: ⚠ TOXICITY — α2-agonist → sedation, bradycardia, hyperglycemia. NOT for cats. Caution in young/diabetic animals (Carpenter p.567). Reversible with atipamezole / yohimbine if toxicosis.

name: Meloxicam
use: Pain, inflammation, antipyretic / fever (1st line dog & exotic)
warn: ⚠ VERIFIED vs Carpenter 6e + Pain Control lecture (May 2026). CARPENTER 6e RABBIT entry is PO ONLY at 1 mg/kg q24h (p.590) — SC route is off-label clinical practice (Plumb's, Cooper 2009). ⚠ Cats: single perioperative dose only — chronic = renal failure. ⚠ NEVER combine with steroids. ⚠ Maintain hydration. ⚠ Reptile doses are mostly single-dose / PK studies — clinical efficacy not fully evaluated.

name: Carprofen

doses.d: 2–4 mg/kg PO; 4 mg/kg SC then 2 mg/kg × 2

warn: ⚠ Carpenter Reptile p.162: "no efficacy data in any reptile species" — dose extrapolated. ⚠ Carpenter Bird p.324: "Caution should be used when administering to Gyps vultures and pigeons" — pigeon PD: 2-10 mg/kg associated with ↑AST/ALT, mottled yellow livers, pale muscle injection sites, histologic kidney/liver lesions. Cats: single perioperative dose only — chronic = renal failure. Idiosyncratic hepatotoxicity in some Labradors.

name: Buprenorphine

doses.f: PK reached human levels but NO analgesia

warn: ⚠ Carpenter Bird p.308: BUPRENORPHINE INEFFECTIVE in African Grey parrots even at human-equivalent plasma levels (PK confirmed) — also failed PD in red-tailed hawks at 0.25 mg/kg. μ-receptor distribution variable in birds. ✓ Effective in American kestrels at 0.1–0.6 mg/kg (6 hr thermal antinociception). Butorphanol (κ-agonist) often preferred in psittacines. ⚠ Pain scores in mice not sufficient post-laparotomy at 0.1 mg/kg.

name: Butorphanol
warn: ⚠ Carpenter Reptile p.151: "Butorphanol... inadequate for analgesia" in reptiles — primarily used as preanesthetic/sedative only. Bird PD: significantly reduced isoflurane ED50 in African greys and cockatoos but NOT in Amazon parrots — species-dependent (Carpenter p.309). Bird harlequin duck: no iso-sparing detected. ✓ FIRST-LINE for BIRD PAIN (e.g., ear-base abscess, fracture, post-op) — κ-opioid receptors are predominant in avian CNS, making butorphanol generally MORE effective than buprenorphine in psittacines and raptors.

name: Tramadol
use: Moderate pain — strongly species-dependent in exotics

doses.f: no analgesic effect; side effects >40 mg/kg

warn: ⚠⚠ AVOID in CHINCHILLA — Carpenter 6e p.579 PD study: NO analgesic effect at 10–40 mg/kg SC, and SEVERE transient neurologic adverse effects at >40 mg/kg. Confirmed by Exotic Pain Control lecture (2026). ⚠ Limited evidence in rabbits — Carpenter (rabbit p.623): "Pharmacokinetic data reported to be variable, data on clinical efficacy lacking". Did NOT show isoflurane-sparing effect at 4.4 mg/kg IV. ⚠ Guinea Pig: no published data. ✓ FIRST-LINE for REPTILE PAIN (e.g., chelonian ear-base abscess, post-op): strongest evidence base in chelonians (loggerhead, red-eared slider, sea turtles) — PK confirms 5–10 mg/kg PO q72h. ✓ Effective in Hispaniolan Amazons + raptors when butorphanol/buprenorphine insufficient. Efficacy debated in dogs. Combine with NSAID for surgical pain.

name: Gabapentin
warn: ⚠ Carpenter Bird p.314: Prairie falcon DEVELOPED neurologic signs + diarrhea at 110 mg/kg (no AE at 82 mg/kg) — keep doses within published ranges. Long-term use in birds for self-mutilation effective. ⚠ Avoid xylitol-containing liquid formulations in dogs (xylitol = hypoglycemia/liver toxicity). Senegal parrot sedation seen day 3 — monitor for over-sedation.

name: Morphine
warn: ⚠ CONTROLLED opioid. Dose-dependent respiratory depression; reduces GI motility (caution in rabbit stasis). Ferret 1 mg/kg SC → emesis/excitability/ptyalism. Species-dependent efficacy (poor in some snakes). Carpenter p.133/547/591/635.

name: Hydromorphone
warn: ⚠ CONTROLLED opioid. Respiratory depression; sedation at higher doses. Efficacy species-dependent (e.g. not effective in cockatiels). Carpenter p.131/282/546/588/635.

name: Methadone
warn: ⚠ CONTROLLED opioid. Per BSAVA Cascade, methadone preferred over morphine in rabbits (rabbit dose from BSAVA p.299). Respiratory depression; species-dependent efficacy. Carpenter p.133/500/546–547.

name: Fentanyl
warn: ⚠ CONTROLLED potent opioid. Marked respiratory depression/bradycardia with IV CRI — monitor closely. Transdermal absorption temperature-dependent in reptiles. SC → hyperactivity in some cockatoos. Carpenter p.131/281/587/635.

name: Flunixin meglumine
warn: ⚠ NSAID — GI ulceration + nephrotoxicity. NOT in dehydrated/hypovolemic patients; ensure hydration; SHORT courses (≤3–5d). Renal lesions reported in birds at low doses; AVOID in Gyps vultures. Carpenter p.131/293/545/587/635.

name: Robenacoxib
use: Acute/chronic pain, antipyretic / fever (cat-safe short course)
warn: ⚠ NSAID — GI/renal/hepatic risk; not for dehydrated/hypotensive patients or with steroids/other NSAIDs. No exotic dose in Carpenter — exotic omitted (do not extrapolate). Cat: empty stomach, limited duration.

name: Acetaminophen (Paracetamol)
warn: ⚠⚠ FATAL IN CATS — cats lack glucuronidation → methemoglobinemia, Heinz-body hemolytic anemia, hepatic necrosis, death. ABSOLUTELY CONTRAINDICATED in cats. Caution in birds (hepatotoxic overdose). Dog only; hepatotoxic on overdose in all species.

name: Amantadine
use: Adjunct for chronic / wind-up / refractory pain (with NSAID or opioid)
warn: ⚠ Adjunct only — weak as a sole analgesic; potentiates other analgesics. Use with NSAID/opioid. Carpenter p.635 (ferret).

name: Aspirin
warn: ⚠ NSAID — GI ulceration/bleeding + renal toxicity; SHORT courses. ⚠ CATS metabolize slowly — extended interval, narrow margin (vet supervision only). Carpenter p.292/544/584/634.

name: Dipyrone (Metamizole)
use: High refractory fever; visceral / spasmodic pain
warn: ⚠ Banned in US/UK (agranulocytosis risk) — available as Novalgina (Brazil) / dipyrone in parts of Asia. Recheck CBC after ~2 days. Strong antipyretic when NSAIDs insufficient.

name: Firocoxib
warn: ⚠ NSAID — GI / renal / hepatic caution; not with steroids or other NSAIDs; ensure hydration. Dog only (cat → robenacoxib). Newer COX-2 selective — fewer GI effects.

name: Ketamine
warn: ⚠ NEVER use alone — Carpenter 6e p.620 (rabbit) explicitly: combination required. Snakes may be permanently aggressive after ketamine anesthesia (Carpenter p.154). Avoid in renal dysfunction. Painful at injection site. Reversal: atipamezole reverses only the α2 component. In Ferrets, doses >30 mg/kg alone → poor muscle relaxation, rough recoveries, convulsions.

name: Dexmedetomidine
warn: Reversal: atipamezole (Antisedan) at 10× the dexmed mg dose IM. Causes profound bradycardia + peripheral vasoconstriction — pulse-ox may read falsely low. Avoid in pre-existing cardiac disease. Note: dexmedetomidine doses are HALF of medetomidine.

name: Midazolam
warn: Reversal: flumazenil 0.01–0.1 mg/kg IV (Carpenter p.643 rabbit). Intranasal route excellent in rabbits + raptors for stress-free sedation. Less painful than diazepam IM; water-soluble; more potent + shorter-acting than diazepam.

name: Propofol

doses.sp: Rabbit (slow IV — sole agent NOT recommended)

warn: Carpenter 6e p.623 (rabbit): "Not recommended as sole agent for anesthesia maintenance". Apnea common — have ventilation ready BEFORE giving. Cardiovascular depression. Heinz-body anemia in cats with repeated daily use (extrapolated from cat literature).

name: Alfaxalone
warn: IM volume can limit use in tiny patients (Alfaxan = 10 mg/mL). Dose-dependent respiratory depression on IV — pre-oxygenate. NO analgesic properties — pair with opioid/local. Excellent first-line injectable for exotics due to wide species safety margin.

name: Diazepam
warn: ⚠ ORAL DIAZEPAM = FATAL hepatic necrosis in cats (within days). Many cases of midazolam superseding diazepam in exotics (Carpenter p.152, p.618). Diazepam contains propylene glycol — irritating SC. ⚠ Drug interaction with ivermectin in reptiles (Carpenter p.152). Reversal: flumazenil 0.01–0.1 mg/kg IV.

name: Acepromazine
warn: ⚠ NO analgesic effect — must combine with opioid/NSAID for painful procedures. Causes hypotension/hypothermia (Carpenter p.615) — AVOID in shock, dehydration, geriatric. AVOID in boxer/giant breeds, MDR1 dogs. Cannot be pharmacologically reversed. Lower end of dose range preferred in rabbits.

name: Lidocaine / Bupivacaine (Local)
use: Local / regional / incisional blocks, dental, multimodal analgesia

doses.d: Lidocaine 1–2 mg/kg (dilute, keep total low); Bupivacaine caution

warn: ⚠⚠ MAX-DOSE TOXICITY is the main danger in small exotics — always calculate total mg and stay under the per-kg ceiling; dilute (e.g., lidocaine with saline, ±1:9 sodium bicarbonate) to get enough volume for tiny patients. ⚠ Bupivacaine is CARDIOTOXIC — NEVER give IV / intravascular; ASPIRATE before injecting (Carpenter: bird bupivacaine TD50 ≈ 1.94 mg/kg IV in chickens). ⚠ Cats & small mammals are more sensitive — use the lower ceiling. Carpenter reptile values: lidocaine 2–5 mg/kg, bupivacaine 1–2 mg/kg (max 4). Toxicity signs: muscle tremor → seizure → bradycardia/arrest.

name: Isoflurane
warn: ⚠ % delivered to effect — NOT mg/kg. Dose-dependent hypotension/respiratory depression; premed lowers MAC. Raptors/macaws prone to iso-induced arrhythmias. Carpenter p.122/282/540/588.

name: Sevoflurane
use: GA induction & maintenance (faster on/off than iso)
warn: ⚠ % delivered to effect — NOT mg/kg. Lower solubility = faster induction/recovery than isoflurane; same dose-dependent cardiopulmonary depression. Carpenter p.590/500/128.

name: Xylazine
use: Sedation, analgesia, muscle relaxation (usually w/ ketamine)
warn: ⚠ Marked cardiopulmonary depression — bradycardia, hypotension, arrhythmias. Reverse with atipamezole or yohimbine. NOT alone in birds (excitement/convulsions/death). Avoid in sick animals. Carpenter p.291/634.

name: Atipamezole

doses.d: Same volume as dexmed (10× dexmed / 5× med in mg)

warn: ⚠ Reversal target = dexmedetomidine/medetomidine (+ xylazine). Rule: same volume as the α2-agonist (5× med or 10× dexmed on mg basis). Give IM in chelonians (IV → severe hypotension). Carpenter p.118–119/275/584.

name: Naloxone
warn: ⚠ Reversal target = opioids (µ-agonists). Also reverses analgesia — sudden pain → catecholamine surge/arrhythmia; avoid right after a painful procedure. Short-acting — repeat prn. Carpenter p.127/289/591/633.

name: Flumazenil
warn: ⚠ Reversal target = midazolam / diazepam. Short-acting — resedation possible, repeat prn. Carpenter p.121/281/587/516.

name: Yohimbine
warn: ⚠ Reversal target = xylazine. Atipamezole usually preferred (reverses all α2). Birds: excitement/mortality at >1 mg/kg. Carpenter p.291/592/634.

name: Tiletamine-Zolazepam
warn: ⚠ Prolonged/rough recoveries; severe respiratory depression possible. ⚠ NEPHROTOXIC in rabbits (renal tubular necrosis) — NOT recommended. ⚠ Do NOT use in sugar gliders (neurologic syndromes & death at 10 mg/kg). Carpenter p.129/290/591/500.

name: Glycopyrrolate
warn: ⚠ PREFERRED over atropine in RABBITS — many rabbits have serum atropinesterase making atropine unreliable (rats too). Slower onset than atropine. Carpenter p.585 (atropinase note)/121/282.

name: Maropitant
warn: SC injection is painful — refrigerate or cool before injection (Carpenter sugar glider note p.533). NK1 antagonist substance P pathway conserved across classes — analgesic adjunct for visceral & arthritic pain (Carpenter rabbit p.621, p.633).

name: Metoclopramide
warn: Avoid in GI obstruction. CNS extrapyramidal signs at high dose (Carpenter bird p.370). Bird PD study (Amazon parrot p.371): 1 mg/kg IM showed NO alterations in motility — may need higher dose.

name: Ondansetron
warn: Reserve for refractory vomiting — maropitant is first-line in most exotics. Expensive.

name: Famotidine
warn: Less potent than PPI (omeprazole) — for mild/moderate ulcer prophylaxis. Maintain hydration. Long-term H2 blockade not as effective as PPI for refractory GERD.

name: Omeprazole
warn: ⚠ Carpenter Rabbit p.633: 20 mg/kg SC q12h is significantly HIGHER than dog/cat standard 0.5–1 mg/kg — note specifically used as "gastroprotective" rather than acid suppression target. Give 30 min before food. Long-term use → vitamin/mineral malabsorption — taper, do NOT stop abruptly.

name: Cisapride
warn: ⚠ COMPOUNDED ONLY in US (off-market; Carpenter p.366, p.621, p.633, p.673 all reference compounding). Contraindicated in GI obstruction. ⚠ Carpenter Reptile p.178: ineffective in desert tortoises at 1 mg/kg.

name: Sucralfate

doses.f: q8h (1 hr before food or other drugs)

warn: ⚠ Give 1 hr BEFORE other PO meds — chelates other drugs (Carpenter bird p.373). Requires acidic pH to activate — avoid concurrent PPI use within 30 min. Bind irreversibly to ulcer site.

name: Lactulose
warn: Does NOT treat liver disease itself — reduces blood ammonia via osmotic + bacterial fermentation (Carpenter bird p.370). Higher doses may cause soft stools (Carpenter ferret p.674). Titrate to 2–3 soft stools/day.

name: Cimetidine
warn: ⚠ Many hepatic CYP450 drug interactions; reduce dose in renal failure; give IV slowly. Carpenter p.673/632/583/178/366.

name: Ranitidine
warn: ⚠ Has prokinetic effect — rule out GI obstruction before use. Carpenter p.634 (rabbit), p.585 (GP/chinchilla).

name: Pantoprazole
warn: ⚠ No exotic dose in Carpenter — exotic species omitted (do not extrapolate). IV PPI option when PO not possible.

name: Cholestyramine
use: Toxin binder for herbivore enterotoxemia (binds clostridial/iota toxins)
warn: ⚠ Separate from other oral drugs (binds them); may cause constipation. Not absorbed → systemic toxicity unlikely. Key adjunct in suspected antibiotic-induced enterotoxemia. Carpenter p.631 (rabbit), p.583 (GP). BSAVA alt rabbit regimen: 0.5 g/kg PO q8–12h.

name: Cyproheptadine
warn: ⚠ May cause sedation or paradoxical excitement; anticholinergic effects. Carpenter p.673 (ferret), p.632 (rabbit).

name: Mirtazapine
use: Appetite stimulant (very common in cats), anti-nausea
warn: ⚠ Cats: use low dose — overdose → serotonin signs/vocalization. Rabbit dose extrapolated from cat (not established; BSAVA p.307). Carpenter p.633 (rabbit).

name: Silymarin (Milk Thistle)
warn: ⚠ Empiric / herbal; efficacy variable. Carpenter p.674/634/585/373/179.

name: Ursodiol (Ursodeoxycholic acid)
warn: ⚠ Contraindicated with complete biliary obstruction. Carpenter p.676 (ferret), p.374 (bird).

name: Bismuth Subsalicylate
use: GI ulcer, antidiarrheal; part of ferret Helicobacter triple therapy
warn: ⚠ Contains salicylate — caution/avoid in cats; darkens stool (mimics melena); avoid prolonged use. Carpenter p.673 (ferret), p.361 (bird).

name: Loperamide
warn: ⚠ Use caution — contraindicated in infectious/toxin diarrhea & GI obstruction; risk with MDR1 mutation (collies). Carpenter p.674/633/584.

name: Meclizine
use: Motion sickness; vestibular disease / head tilt
warn: Less sedating than diphenhydramine; lasts 12–24h. Useful for E. cuniculi vestibular disease / rolling + head tilt in rabbits.

name: Chlorpheniramine
warn: Less sedating than diphenhydramine. Useful in cats needing combined anti-pruritic + anti-nausea.

name: Furosemide

doses.d: 2–3 mg/kg, then 1–2 mg/kg PO q12h long-term

warn: ⚠ Carpenter Bird p.368: LORIES are EXTREMELY SENSITIVE to furosemide — use lowest dose, monitor closely. Narrow safety margin in all birds. ⚠ RENAL PORTAL SYSTEM in birds & reptiles: NEVER inject in caudal half (legs/tail) — drug is filtered by kidneys before reaching systemic circulation → sub-therapeutic + nephrotoxicity. Always inject in CRANIAL half (pectoral / forelimb). ⚠ Reptiles lack loop of Henle — Carpenter p.178: "may effect via other mechanisms". Dehydration + hypokalemia + visceral gout risk.

name: Pimobendan
warn: ⚠ Conflicting bird doses in Carpenter: psittacine 0.25 mg/kg standard q12h (p.371) vs 6–10 mg/kg PD (p.372). Begin LOW (0.25), titrate up. Administration with food may reduce bioavailability (Sugar Glider note p.534).

name: Enalapril

doses.f: q24–48h (start low — hypotension; avoid with renal disease)

warn: ⚠ Ferret p.668: Do NOT use with concurrent renal disease. Carpenter rabbit p.628 notes "monitor for hypotensive side effects". Start LOW, monitor BP + electrolytes + creatinine.

name: Benazepril
use: CHF, hypertension, CRD proteinuria — less nephrotoxic than enalapril
warn: Carpenter Ferret p.668: "less nephrotoxic than enalapril" — preferred in patients with renal impairment. Monitor BP + electrolytes.

name: Atenolol
warn: Carpenter Rabbit p.627 notes use "in conjunction with methimazole for maximum effect on thyroid hormones" — rabbit hyperthyroidism is documented but rare. Monitor HR + BP. Do not stop abruptly — taper.

name: Diltiazem
use: HCM, supraventricular tachyarrhythmia, atrial fibrillation, hypertension
warn: ⚠ Negative inotrope/chronotrope — avoid in significant bradycardia / AV block / systolic dysfunction; monitor HR/BP. Carpenter p.636 (ferret), p.596 (rabbit).

name: Amlodipine
warn: ⚠ Monitor for hypotension; titrate to blood pressure. Carpenter p.636 (ferret), p.333 (parrots).

name: Sildenafil
warn: ⚠ No exotic dose in Carpenter — exotic omitted (do not extrapolate). Hypotension risk; avoid with nitrates. Confirm pulmonary hypertension (echo) before use.

name: Clopidogrel
warn: ⚠ No exotic dose in Carpenter — exotic omitted (do not extrapolate). Bleeding risk; stop before elective surgery. First-line for feline ATE.

name: Spironolactone
warn: ⚠ Potassium-sparing — hyperkalemia risk, esp. with ACE inhibitors / renal disease; monitor K⁺. Carpenter p.596 (rabbit), p.341 (bird), p.548 (chinchilla).

name: Digoxin
warn: ⚠ NARROW therapeutic index — toxicity (anorexia, vomiting, ataxia, arrhythmia); contraindicated with renal/hepatic disease; monitor serum digoxin + electrolytes + ECG. Carpenter p.636 (ferret), p.596 (rabbit).

name: Epinephrine
warn: Carpenter 6e CPR doses are HIGHER than dog/cat: Rabbit 0.2–0.4 mg/kg (p.611, verified), Ferret cardiac arrest 0.2 mg/kg (p.668). 1:1 000 (1 mg/mL) for IM. Dilute to 1:10 000 (0.1 mg/mL) for IV in small mammals only. Bird table on p.421 specifies 1 mg/mL stock. ⚠ NOTE: BSAVA / standard small-animal CPR uses LOWER doses (0.01 mg/kg low-dose → 0.1 mg/kg high-dose) — Carpenter exotic figures are higher; follow your CPR protocol.

name: Atropine

doses.d: 0.1–0.5 mg/kg (repeat q10–20 min due to esterase)

warn: ⚠ Rabbits + some rats produce atropinesterase → atropine may be ineffective (Carpenter 6e p.611, p.581). Use glycopyrrolate instead in rabbits when possible. Repeat dosing q10–20 min often needed in rabbits.

name: Doxapram
warn: Carpenter 6e p.643 (rabbit): "side effects can be significant (cardiac arrhythmias, muscle fasciculation, seizures), therefore its use should be limited to patients that cannot be intubated or otherwise allow control of ventilation". Limited first-line use — prefer mechanical ventilation if possible.

name: Dexamethasone
warn: ⚠ Use SPARINGLY in rabbits & guinea pigs — high immunosuppression risk, GI ulceration, potential for fatal outcome with even short courses. Never combine with NSAID. Avoid in suspected GI bleeding, diabetes. Single shock dose only — do NOT continue daily.

name: Prednisolone
warn: ⚠ Rabbits + GP: immunosuppression risk — use lowest effective dose. Rabbits are extremely steroid-sensitive (even low/single doses → profound immunosuppression + hepatic lipidosis/vacuolar hepatopathy); GP & ferret are relatively steroid-resistant. ⚠ BIRDS: prolonged corticosteroids strongly predispose to fatal invasive aspergillosis — avoid chronic use; also markedly immunosuppressive in birds/reptiles. Never combine with NSAID. Always TAPER, never stop abruptly. CPR/shock formulation = prednisolone sodium succinate (10 mg/mL); oral PO formulation different.

name: Diphenhydramine
use: Allergy, anaphylaxis, mild sedation, motion sickness
warn: Pre-medicate before vaccines / injections in patients with prior reaction history. For acute anaphylaxis, give epinephrine FIRST — diphenhydramine is adjunct.

name: Calcium Gluconate 10%
warn: IV must be SLOW with ECG monitoring — bradyarrhythmia risk (asystole if pushed). Extravasation causes severe skin necrosis. Dilute 1:1 for SC. Concentration in Carpenter 6e p.421 bird table = 100 mg/mL (10%).

name: Oxytocin
use: Dystocia / egg binding — NON-obstructive only, after calcium

doses.f: ~1 hr after calcium; best in chelonians

warn: ⚠⚠ ONLY for NON-OBSTRUCTIVE dystocia / egg binding — rule out obstruction (palpation/imaging + adequate pelvic canal/shell width) FIRST. Oxytocin against an obstruction can rupture the uterus/oviduct. ⚠ Give AFTER calcium (Carpenter: ~1 hr post-Ca); hypocalcemia blunts uterine response. Reptiles: works well in chelonians, less in snakes/lizards (Carpenter reptile ch.); birds index p.297–302, ferret p.636–637. Use repeat doses with caution.

name: Vitamin K1

doses.d: 2.5 mg/kg SC, then 1–2.5 mg/kg PO divided q8–12h × 5–7d
2.5–5 mg/kg SC, then 2.5 mg/kg PO × 3–4 wk
5 mg/kg SC, then 2.5 mg/kg PO × 3 wk
2.5 mg/kg SC, then 5 mg/kg PO × ≥14 days
0.2–2.2 mg/kg q4–8h until stable, then q24h × 14–28d

warn: ⚠ AVOID IV — anaphylactoid reactions reported. SC then PO with fatty meal optimal absorption. Vitamin K is fat soluble — give PO formulations in oil (Carpenter rabbit p.635). For rodenticide: ALWAYS treat full 28 days for 2nd-gen (brodifacoum, etc.) and verify with PT recheck after taper.

name: Dextrose 50%
warn: ⚠ MUST be diluted before IV — undiluted 50% is hyperosmolar/phlebitic/necrotizing; NEVER SC or perivascular. ⚠ Insulinoma: large boluses provoke rebound hyperinsulinemia → worse hypoglycemia; give minimum to control signs. Carpenter p.330 (bird), p.501 (sugar glider).

name: Activated Charcoal

doses.d: 1–4 g/kg; repeat 1–2 g/kg for recycled toxins

warn: ⚠ NOT for caustic/petroleum ingestion or unprotected airway (aspiration risk); ensure hydration. Avoid repeat cathartic-containing doses. Carpenter p.640 (ferret), p.600 (rabbit), p.552 (GP).

name: Apomorphine
use: Induce emesis after recent oral toxin ingestion

doses.d: 0.03 mg/kg IV; 0.04 IM/SC; or conjunctival (¼ tab, rinse after)

warn: ⚠ DOGS (and ferrets) only. Do NOT use in cats (use dexmedetomidine). NEVER in rabbits/guinea pigs/chinchillas/rodents — they physiologically CANNOT vomit (emetics ineffective/harmful). May cause protracted emesis, sedation, respiratory depression. Carpenter p.640 (ferret).

name: Insulin (NPH / Lente)
warn: ⚠⚠ ALL doses empirical — MUST adjust based on serial blood glucose curves. Carpenter Reptile p.169: "doses administered ICe may take 24-48 hr before a response is noted". Reptile diabetes is rare and poorly characterized — consult specialist before initiating. Bird diabetes presents differently (chronic hyperglycemia tolerated better than mammals). ⚠ Hypoglycemia from overdose is the #1 acute toxicity — keep dextrose ready, never push BG to <60 mg/dL initially.

name: Insulin Glargine
warn: ⚠ Glargine should NOT be mixed with other insulins. Highest remission rate in cats when combined with low-carb diet. Refrigerate; do not freeze.

name: Levothyroxine
warn: Cats rarely hypothyroid — verify before treating. ⚠ Carpenter Reptile p.169: Levothyroxine used in debilitated tortoises to STIMULATE FEEDING — not always for hypothyroidism. Monitor T4 levels q4–6 wk during titration.

name: Methimazole
warn: ⚠ Hepatotoxicity, blood dyscrasia, facial pruritus possible. CBC + T4 q3 wk during titration. ⚠ Carpenter Reptile p.179: "limited effectiveness" for snake hyperthyroidism. Guinea pig hyperthyroidism increasingly recognized — methimazole + atenolol combo (Carpenter p.633 rabbit) most effective.

name: Leuprolide
use: Ferret adrenal disease (palliative); avian reproductive control
warn: ⚠ Initial hormonal flare before suppression. Does NOT resolve adrenal tumors in ferrets — palliative only, lifetime therapy; must be aliquoted/frozen. Carpenter p.638 (ferret), p.312 (parrots), p.552 (GP).

name: Deslorelin
use: Ferret adrenal disease (mainstay); reversible sterilization; avian repro suppression

doses.d: 4.7 mg implant (10–18 mo) or 9.4 mg (16–48 mo)

warn: ⚠ Initial hormonal flare before suppression; does NOT cure ferret adrenal tumor (controls signs only). Only the 4.7 mg implant is widely available. Carpenter p.637 (ferret), p.297 (bird), p.600 (rabbit), p.551 (GP).

name: Desmopressin (DDAVP)
use: Central diabetes insipidus; von Willebrand factor release

doses.d: 1–4 drops (≈1.5–4 µg) conjunctival; or 0.01–0.05 mg/kg SC; vWD 1 µg/kg pre-op
4.6 µg/kg (African grey, central DI; titrate up to 24)

warn: ⚠ Overdose / excess water intake → hyponatremia / water intoxication; titrate to response, monitor electrolytes. Carpenter p.298 (African grey).

name: Vitamin B Complex
warn: ⚠ Carpenter Reptile p.174: "use with caution as B6 toxicity may occur" at high doses. ⚠ Sugar Glider p.534: stings on injection — best to inject into pocket of SC fluids. Dilute small animal formulation. Piscivorous reptiles (eating thawed fish) → high thiaminase exposure → must supplement thiamine.

name: Vitamin C (Ascorbic Acid)
use: Guinea pig scurvy, rabbit supportive, supportive
warn: ⚠ Guinea pigs CANNOT synthesize Vitamin C — daily dietary supply is MANDATORY. Hedgehog Carpenter p.552: "1 g/L drinking water NOT recommended; alternative routes preferred (oral pills or powder)". Reptile use empirical — for hypovitaminosis C, stomatitis, skin slough in snakes.

name: Vitamin A
warn: ⚠ OVERDOSE → hypervitaminosis A (epidermal sloughing in chelonians, hepatic toxicity in others); Carpenter 6e p.174 notes greater risk with the aqueous parenteral formulation. Use lowest effective dose.

name: Iron Dextran
warn: ⚠ Carpenter Bird p.349: USE CAUTIOUSLY in species prone to IRON STORAGE DISEASE — toucans, mynahs, starlings, birds of paradise, other passerines. Confirm true iron deficiency before treating. Injection-site reactions common — split large doses.

name: Cyanocobalamin (Vitamin B12)
use: B12 deficiency, anemia, cobalamin supplementation, appetite
warn: ⚠ Very safe (water-soluble). Carpenter p.318 (bird), p.142 (reptile).

name: PHMB + Tris-EDTA
use: Wound flush, mouth wash, nebulization, nasal flush — extra-label exotic use

doses.sp: Reptile (Carpenter F10 = PHMB/benzalkonium chelonian nasal flush)

warn: ⚠ The proprietary brand PHMB-Tris-EDTA combinations are NOT in Carpenter 6e directly — only F10 polyhexamethylene biguanide (chelonian nasal flush at 1:250, Carpenter p.141). Doses for wounds/oral are extra-label clinical practice (KUVTH protocols Duangurai 2026). Refrigerate diluted preparations; discard after 7d.

name: Chlorhexidine Gluconate
use: Skin prep, wound lavage, oral surgery, dental rinse

doses.f: q2–7d prn (not sole agent for dermatophytes)

warn: ⚠ Carpenter Bird p.260: "topical application may be fatal to nun (mannikin) and parrot finches" — AVOID topical use in small passerines. ⚠ OTOTOXIC if reaches middle ear — never deep ear canal flush. ⚠ TOXIC to fish & amphibians (permeable skin / gill absorption). Surgical alcohol & detergents in chlorhexidine scrub are OCULAR TOXIC — use povidone-iodine 0.2% for ocular prep instead.

name: Povidone-Iodine
use: Skin prep, fungal lesions, shell rot, ocular preop

doses.d: Ocular prep: 0.2% solution (1:50 dilution from 10% stock)

warn: ⚠ CONCENTRATED solution INHIBITS wound healing — always dilute to 0.05% or less. Inactivated by organic debris — debride first. Reptile shells must dry before returning to water (Carpenter p.139). Surgical scrub form (with detergent) is OCULAR TOXIC — use solution only for eye prep.

name: Silver Sulfadiazine (SSD)
use: Burns, deep wounds, scale rot, Amazon foot necrosis

doses.sp: Reptile (all species — wound, burn, oral cavity)

warn: Excellent activity vs Pseudomonas (Carpenter Bird p.273: "specifically for burn wounds; ulcers"). Can IMPAIR re-epithelialization in delicate species → switch to PHMB/HOCl once granulation present. Avoid large surface area in cats (sulfonamide absorption).

name: Hypochlorous Acid (HOCl)
use: Chronic wound, eye flush, respiratory environment mist
warn: ⚠ NOT in Carpenter 6e — all doses are proprietary label / clinical practice (off-label). Non-toxic if licked or ingested — ideal for self-grooming exotics. SHORT shelf life once opened (4–6 wks). Refrigerate; discard if cloudy.

name: F10 SC / F10 SCXD
use: Avian aspergillosis nebulization, environmental disinfection, wound
warn: Workhorse for exotic clinic. Nebulization F10 commonly used for aspergillosis prevention (Carpenter Bird p.336, raptor). Non-irritant at recommended dilution; full strength is corrosive — verify dilution before use.

name: Amphotericin B (Nebulization)

doses.d: 1 mg/kg, diluted to 1 mL sterile water

warn: TOPICAL/INHALED use only — systemic IV amphotericin highly nephrotoxic. Often combined with itraconazole or voriconazole PO. Carpenter Reptile p.140: also documented IV 0.5 mg/kg q48–72h (systemic) with caution — administer slowly.

name: Tobramycin (Ophthalmic)
warn: Loading dose protocol (q5–10 min × 1h) for infected corneal ulcer. Always 1 drop only. Wait ≥ 5 min between different eye drops.

name: Ciprofloxacin (Ophthalmic)
warn: Carpenter Rabbit p.629: Good penetration into aqueous + vitreous humor. Can deposit white crystalline precipitate on healing ulcer base — benign but may obscure cytology.

name: Atropine 1% (Ophthalmic)
use: Uveitis ciliary spasm pain relief, mydriasis for exam

doses.d: AVOID for mydriasis — iris is striated muscle
AVOID for mydriasis — iris is striated muscle

warn: ⚠ Carpenter Rabbit p.628: "may be ineffective in rabbits with pigmented irises or those that produce atropinase". Systemic effects possible. ⚠ BIRDS & REPTILES iris is striated (skeletal) muscle — atropine does NOT cause mydriasis. Use topical d-tubocurarine, vecuronium, or anesthesia. Bitter → hypersalivation in cats; prefer ointment.

name: Tropicamide 1% (Ophthalmic)
warn: ⚠ Carpenter 6e lists tropicamide 1% for rabbits (mydriasis, p.631). Ineffective in birds & reptiles (striated iris muscle — use a neuromuscular blocker e.g. d-tubocurarine / vecuronium topically for mydriasis instead).

name: Prednisolone Acetate 1% (Ophthalmic)
warn: ⚠ Carpenter Rabbit p.630: "rabbits are a corticosteroid-sensitive species" — even topical use can cause immunosuppression. ⚠ DO NOT USE if corneal ulcer (fluorescein +) — perforation risk. Avoid in reptiles. Always shake suspension well.

name: Dexamethasone 0.1% (Ophthalmic)

doses.sp: Rabbit (PK study, corneal distribution > oral pred)
doses.d: 0.1%, 1 drop q12h × 2d, then 1 drop on day 3

warn: ⚠ Contraindicated with corneal ulcer. Carpenter Rabbit p.629: corneal distribution superior to oral prednisolone. Often combined with Neomycin+Polymyxin in "NPD" ophth ointment.

name: Diclofenac 0.1% (Ophthalmic)
use: Post-op, corneal abrasion, recurrent erosion, blepharitis
warn: Safer alternative to corticosteroids in diabetic/cushingoid patients. Can cause corneal melting if pre-existing collagenase activity — recheck q24h initially.

name: Cyclosporine 0.2% (Ophthalmic)
use: KCS (dry eye), chronic superficial keratitis, pannus, dacryoadenitis
warn: Lacrimostimulant — restores tear production over 3–6 weeks. Carpenter rabbit p.629 documents both 0.05% Restasis (autoimmune dacryoadenitis) and 0.2% Optimmune (tear production / prevents post-op precorneal membranous occlusion). Safe for chronic use.

name: Tacrolimus 0.02% (Ophthalmic)
warn: ⚠ NOT in Carpenter 6e exotic ophthalmic tables — dose extrapolated from canine. More potent than cyclosporine — use when CsA fails. Compounded only. Wear gloves (human carcinogen warning).

name: Tetracaine 0.5% (Ophthalmic)
use: Tonometry, corneal FB removal, suture removal
warn: ⚠ NOT specifically in Carpenter ophthalmic tables. DIAGNOSTIC USE ONLY — repeated use causes epithelial necrosis and inhibits healing. Never dispense for home use. Transient stinging expected.

name: Acyclovir / Ganciclovir (Ophthalmic)
warn: ⚠ NOT in Carpenter 6e rabbit ophthalmic table specifically — extrapolated. Cats with refractory FHV: combine topical antiviral + oral Famciclovir 90 mg/kg PO q8–12h. Trifluridine + idoxuridine both effective in rabbit herpes keratitis model.

name: Hyaluronate (Ophthalmic)
warn: ⚠ NOT in Carpenter 6e — universal supportive. Preservative-free formulations safe for chronic q1–2h use. More viscous than methylcellulose / PVA — longer corneal retention.

name: Latanoprost 0.005% (Ophthalmic)
warn: ⚠ Carpenter does not list latanoprost in rabbit ophthalmic table — newer agent extrapolated. Causes intense miosis, mild conjunctival hyperemia, iris darkening. Avoid in uveitis.

name: Timolol 0.5% (Ophthalmic)
warn: ⚠ Systemic absorption can cause bradycardia, bronchospasm — avoid in cardiac dz, asthma. Often combined with carbonic anhydrase inhibitor (dorzolamide) in COSOPT.

name: Dorzolamide 2% (Ophthalmic)
warn: Combined with timolol (COSOPT) is most effective topical antiglaucoma. Burning on instillation common — does not indicate harm.

name: Vincristine
use: Lymphoma, leukemia, transmissible venereal tumor, TVT
warn: ⚠⚠ RABBITS HIGHLY SUSCEPTIBLE → GI STASIS — start at lowest dose, prophylactic prokinetic + CC feeding mandatory. ⚠ Carpenter Rabbit p.627: "neurotoxic effects of vincristine well studied in rabbits". ⚠ EXTRAVASATION = severe tissue necrosis — use central line or fresh catheter, flush with saline before & after. PPE required.

name: Cyclophosphamide
warn: ⚠ Carpenter Rabbit p.626: "repeat cyclophosphamide injections have been associated with RENAL FAILURE in rabbits". STERILE HEMORRHAGIC CYSTITIS — give in morning, force diuresis. Bone marrow nadir 7–10d post-dose; check CBC before each cycle. PPE required.

name: Doxorubicin

doses.d: 1 mg/kg × 2 tx, then q14d × 2, then q21d × 2

warn: ⚠⚠ Carpenter Rabbit p.626: 3 mg/kg (50 mg/m²) q7d × 10 wk in MALE rabbits caused APLASTIC ANEMIA, significant body weight loss, nephrotoxicity, and HIGH PREMATURE MORTALITY. ⚠ CUMULATIVE CARDIOTOXICITY — max 180–240 mg/m² lifetime in dogs; echo baseline + q150 mg/m². ⚠ NEPHROTOXIC in cats. ⚠ Severe extravasation necrosis. Premedicate with diphenhydramine + dexamethasone. PPE required.

name: L-Asparaginase

doses.d: 400 IU/kg SC q7d OR 10,000 IU/m² SC/IM q21d

doses.sp: Bird — Black swan (with lomustine, minimal response)

warn: ⚠ Carpenter Rabbit p.626: Single IV dose at 10,000 IU/kg induced "hyperinsulinemia insulin-resistant diabetic syndrome" in rabbits — AVOID IV route. ⚠ ANAPHYLAXIS on repeat dosing — pre-treat diphenhydramine 2 mg/kg IM 30 min before, observe 30 min post. Coagulopathy reported.

name: Cytarabine (Ara-C)

doses.d: 50 mg/m² SC q12h × 2d OR 100 mg/m² CRI over 24h

warn: ⚠ Carpenter 6e ferret lymphoma protocol III lists cytarabine 300 mg/m² SC × 2 days (p.682) — the 30 mg/m² figure here is a conservative canine-extrapolated alternative; verify the full protocol before use. Marrow suppression — CBC pre-dose. PPE required.

name: Lomustine (CCNU)
use: Mast cell tumor, lymphoma, brain tumors
warn: ⚠⚠ Carpenter Rabbit p.626: rabbit DIED of renal failure 21 days after 2ND treatment at 50 mg/m². Hepatotoxicity, nephrotoxicity, neutropenia — monitor ALT/ALP + BUN/creat + CBC before EACH dose; consider SAMe/silymarin prophylaxis. Delayed marrow nadir 14–21d. PPE required.

name: Terbutaline
use: Bronchospasm, lower airway / obstructive respiratory disease, dyspnea
warn: ⚠ Carpenter 6e: parenteral/nebulized dose is ~0.01 mg/kg; the higher 5 mg/kg PO value (rodent chapter) reflects terbutaline's very low oral bioavailability — DO NOT apply the oral mg/kg figure parenterally. May cause tachycardia (esp. birds). Useful for guinea-pig "asthma-like" bronchospasm and avian/reptile lower-airway disease (often combined with nebulization).

name: Aminophylline
warn: ⚠ Narrow therapeutic index — overdose causes excitement, tremor, tachyarrhythmia, seizures. Helps diaphragm contractility in small mammals. Synergistic with β2-agonists (terbutaline) but additive cardiac stimulation.

name: Theophylline
warn: ⚠ Narrow therapeutic index (like aminophylline) — monitor for excitement, insomnia/restlessness, tachycardia, GI upset. Avoid combining with fluoroquinolones (enrofloxacin raises theophylline levels). Use with caution in cardiac disease.

name: Levamisole
warn: ⚠ Narrow safety margin — overdose causes nicotinic signs (tremor, salivation, paralysis). ⚠ Carpenter: may cause PARALYSIS in some amphibians/reptiles at suggested doses (caffeine or physostigmine may reverse). Also used as an immunostimulant at low doses.

name: Paromomycin
warn: ⚠ Not absorbed from the gut — acts in the intestinal lumen (ideal for Cryptosporidium, which has no fully curative drug). ⚠ Carpenter ferret note: use with caution — severe renal disease possible if mucosa is damaged and drug is absorbed. Reptile/bird doses src: exotic lecture (verify locally).

name: Amprolium

doses.d: 100 mg/kg in food or water (or 50–100 mg/L water)

warn: ⚠ Works by competitively blocking thiamine (vitamin B1) uptake by the coccidia — do NOT co-supplement high-dose thiamine (reduces efficacy). Prolonged high doses can cause thiamine deficiency in the host. Coccidiostatic (slows, does not sterilize) — pair with hygiene.

name: Ronidazole
use: Flagellated protozoa — Giardia, Trichomonas, Cochlosoma, Hexamita
warn: ⚠ Nitroimidazole (related to metronidazole) — preferred for avian Giardia/Trichomonas where metronidazole is less effective. Neurotoxic if overdosed (like metronidazole). Handle with gloves (potential mutagen).

name: Chloroquine
warn: ⚠ Generally combined with PRIMAQUINE for avian Plasmodium/Haemoproteus/Leucocytozoon (chloroquine clears erythrocytic stages, primaquine the tissue stages). ⚠ Carpenter: 10 mg/kg PO × 7 d was INEFFECTIVE against reptile haemogregarine blood parasites — do not rely on it in reptiles. Overdose cardiotoxic.

name: Ketoprofen
warn: ⚠⚠ Carpenter: AVOID in Gyps vultures — mortalities reported at clinical doses (5 mg/kg PO killed 7/11 Cape Griffon vultures), like diclofenac. ⚠ Rats prone to GI ulceration/bleeding/erosions. More COX-1 activity (more GI/renal risk) than meloxicam — short courses, ensure hydration, never combine with another NSAID or steroid.

name: Piroxicam
use: Chronic osteoarthritis pain; metronomic anti-cancer adjunct (TCC, SCC)
warn: ⚠ Oxicam NSAID with COX-2 / anti-angiogenic activity — used both for osteoarthritis pain AND as a metronomic anti-cancer adjunct (transitional cell carcinoma, squamous cell carcinoma). High GI-ulceration risk — give with food ± gastroprotectant; monitor renal values. Do not combine with steroids/other NSAIDs.

name: Tolfenamic Acid
use: Pain / fever / inflammation; pulse dosing; "vulture-safe" NSAID
warn: ⚠ Carpenter limits to ~3 consecutive daily doses (designed for pulse dosing — long anti-inflammatory effect outlasts plasma levels). One of the few NSAIDs SAFE in Gyps vultures (unlike diclofenac/ketoprofen) — relevant for raptor/scavenger work. Standard GI/renal NSAID cautions.

name: Grapiprant
use: Osteoarthritis pain (GI-sparing alternative to classic NSAIDs)
warn: ⚠ Emerging drug — exotic data is LIMITED (src: exotic analgesia lecture, single rabbit PK study; dog dose from Plumb's). Piprant class blocks only the EP4 pain/inflammation receptor, so theoretically more GI-sparing than COX-inhibitor NSAIDs. Give on an empty stomach in dogs. Verify dose/interval before clinical use in exotics.

name: Natamycin 5% (Ophthalmic)

doses.f: q1–2h initially, taper to q6–8h over weeks

warn: ⚠ The ONLY commercially marketed ophthalmic antifungal (US/EU). First-line for filamentous fungal corneal ulcers (Aspergillus, Fusarium) and yeast. Shake well; poor corneal penetration so dose frequently early. Pairs with the Keratomycosis disease entry. Stop any topical steroid (feeds fungus).

name: Trifluridine 1% (Ophthalmic)
use: Herpesviral keratitis / keratoconjunctivitis (FHV-1 and other herpesviruses)
warn: ⚠ Virostatic — must be dosed frequently (≥5×/day) to work; epithelial toxicity with prolonged use. Effective in a rabbit herpes-simplex keratitis model. Alternatives: cidofovir, idoxuridine, ganciclovir/aciclovir. Do NOT use steroids on an active dendritic ulcer.

name: Pilocarpine 1–2% (Ophthalmic)
warn: ⚠ Stings on application; can cause miosis, brow-ache, and (orally for KCS) cholinergic GI signs (salivation, vomiting, diarrhea) — titrate the oral dose carefully. Avoid in uveitis and anterior-lens-luxation glaucoma. Largely superseded by topical CAIs/β-blockers for glaucoma but still useful for neurogenic dry eye.

name: Phenylephrine 2.5% (Ophthalmic)
use: Diagnostic mydriasis; Horner syndrome localization; surgical vasoconstriction
warn: ⚠ Use the 2.5% strength — the 10% concentration is TOXIC to the corneal endothelium (corneal edema) and can cause systemic HYPERTENSION via absorption. Not an effective mydriatic in the cat, cow or horse (use tropicamide). Useful to localize Horner syndrome (denervation hypersensitivity → faster dilation).

name: Olopatadine 0.1% (Ophthalmic)
warn: Dual-acting H1-antagonist + mast-cell stabilizer for seasonal allergic or eosinophilic conjunctivitis (alternatives: sodium cromoglycate, lodoxamide). Well tolerated; for chronic allergic ocular surface disease rather than infection.

name: Diazoxide
warn: ⚠ First-line MEDICAL control for ferret insulinoma when surgery is declined/incomplete — inhibits pancreatic insulin release + promotes gluconeogenesis to hold blood glucose up. Start low (5 mg/kg q12h) and titrate to effect; expensive. Side-effects: anorexia, vomiting, sodium/fluid retention. Pair with frequent feeding ± prednisolone. Not a cure — the insulinoma still grows.

name: Carboplatin
use: Carcinoma, osteosarcoma, SCC, mesothelioma (less nephrotoxic than cisplatin)
warn: ⚠⚠ Preferred platinum agent in small exotics because — unlike CISPLATIN, which causes FATAL pulmonary edema in cats and is highly nephrotoxic — carboplatin is far less nephrotoxic and better tolerated. Still myelosuppressive: CBC before each dose, nadir ~1–2 wk. Cytotoxic — PPE + safe-handling/disposal required. Dose small exotics by body weight (mg/kg) per Carpenter, not the canine mg/m².

name: Pralidoxime (2-PAM)
warn: ⚠ For ORGANOPHOSPHATE poisoning — give WITH atropine (atropine handles muscarinic SLUDGE signs; 2-PAM frees the enzyme + reverses nicotinic muscle signs). Most useful early (<24–48h, before "aging" of the OP-enzyme bond). NOT effective / not needed for carbamates (reversible) — and may be detrimental in carbaryl toxicosis. Slow IV (rapid → tachycardia/laryngospasm).

name: Acetylcysteine (NAC)
use: Paracetamol (acetaminophen) toxicosis; also mucolytic; xylitol/hepatic support
warn: ⚠ Antidote of choice for ACETAMINOPHEN/PARACETAMOL toxicosis (replenishes glutathione → detoxifies NAPQI). ⚠ CATS are exquisitely sensitive to paracetamol (glucuronidation deficiency) → methemoglobinemia + Heinz-body anemia + facial/paw edema; start NAC ASAP. Give the loading dose then q6h; IV must be slow + filtered. Pair with supportive care (± SAMe, vitamin C).

name: Methocarbamol
use: Tremors — pyrethroid/permethrin, tremorgenic mycotoxin, metaldehyde, strychnine; capture myopathy
warn: ⚠ First-line for the muscle TREMORS of permethrin/pyrethroid toxicosis (esp. CATS exposed to dog spot-ons) and tremorgenic mycotoxins. Give slow IV to effect — too fast → recumbency/apnea. Does NOT control true seizures (use diazepam for those). Decontaminate skin (bathe) for dermal exposure.

name: Calcium EDTA (CaNa₂EDTA)
use: Lead & zinc toxicosis (commonest in birds + dogs)
warn: ⚠ Chelates lead & zinc (the classic exotic/avian heavy-metal toxicoses — coins, galvanized wire, fishing weights, paint). ⚠ Ensure the patient is HYDRATED + the GI metal is removed first (chelating with metal still in gut ↑ absorption). Nephrotoxic — give diluted SC, monitor renal values, cycle 5-on/5-off for long courses. Dilute before injection.

name: Penicillamine (D-penicillamine)
warn: ⚠ Oral chelator — useful for outpatient/long-course lead chelation or copper-storage hepatopathy (e.g. Bedlington/Labrador, copper-accumulating species). Give on an EMPTY stomach (food ↓ absorption). GI upset/vomiting + anorexia common — give with anti-emetic; ensure gut is free of metal first.

name: Fomepizole (4-MP)

doses.d: 20 mg/kg load, then 15 mg/kg at 12 & 24h, then 5 mg/kg at 36h

warn: ⚠ ETHYLENE GLYCOL antidote — blocks alcohol dehydrogenase so the glycol is excreted before it becomes toxic oxalate/glycolate. Effective ONLY if given EARLY (dog <8–12h; cat <3h — cats need the much higher dose). After that, it is too late (calcium-oxalate AKI already established) → focus on dialysis/supportive. Ethanol CRI is the cheaper alternative antidote (but more sedation/metabolic effects).

name: Succimer (DMSA)
warn: ⚠ Oral lead/zinc chelator — better GI tolerance + less nephrotoxicity than CaEDTA, and oral route suits outpatient/avian cases. Often used to FOLLOW initial CaEDTA injectable chelation. Ensure the metallic foreign body is removed from the GI tract first (endoscopy/surgery) or chelation just ↑ absorption.
